elements.sent-2411-006: Over the last two decades, many approaches to quantum spin glasses have been explored in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-007: The most popular approach is the so-called quantum ferromagnet model, which can be viewed as a quantum analogue of the Heisenberg model, with classical three-dimensional spin vectors replaced by quantum spins represented by Pauli matrices \cite{baldwin2017clustering,cugliandolo2004effects .}
elements.sent-2411-008: Another prominent example is the fermionic Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) model, known for its maximal chaos and widely studied as an effective quantum model for black holes, with applications in condensed matter physics and quantum gravity \cite{blake2021systems,chowdhury2022sachdev,rosenhaus2019introduction,gurau2018prescription,dartois2019conformality .}
elements.sent-2411-009: This paper investigates a third, less common model, sometimes called the quantum spherical $p$-spin-glass model, which describes the behavior of a quantum particle moving in a $N$-dimensional random energy landscape \cite{biroli2001quantum,rokni2004dynamical .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Specifically, we consider a "2+p" random energy landscape, where the coupling constants are entries from both a random Gaussian matrix and a random tensor of rank $p$.
elements.sent-2411-011: The corresponding classical problem of spherically constrained soft spins with "2+p" interactions can be solved "exactly" using replica symmetry breaking theory \cite{crisanti2006spherical .}
elements.sent-2411-012: In this work, we extend our previous investigations \cite{lahoche2024largetimeeffectivekinetics using functional renormalization group (RG) formalism \cite{Berges,Delamotte_2012 .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Our approach employs an unconventional RG scheme based on coarse-graining over the empirical spectrum of matrix-like disorder, which converges to the deterministic Wigner spectrum in the large $N$ limit.
elements.sent-2411-014: A distinctive feature of this RG approach is that the canonical dimensions depend on the RG scale and on other two-point observables, such as the mass.

elements.sent-2411-043: In this section, we define the model, conventions, and tools that we will use throughout the rest of this paper.

elements.sent-2411-046: We consider a Euclidean (non-relativistic) quantum particle in a space of dimension $N$, moving through a rough landscape characterized by the combination of two types of disorder: one represented by a random Gaussian matrix $K$, and the other by a Gaussian random tensor $J$ of size $q > 2$.

elements.sent-2411-106: Since the pioneering work of Wilson and Kadanoff \cite{Wilson, kadanoff1966scaling , various forms of the RG have been developed for different contexts, intersecting with probability and information theories \cite{gordon2021relevance, apenko2012information, beny2015information, pessoa2018exact, cotler2023renormalization .}}
elements.sent-2411-107: RG appears to be a general concept in physics rather than a topic confined to QFT or condensed matter theory.
elements.sent-2411-108: Notably, RG enables the exploration of effective infrared (IR) physics by progressively integrating out short-wavelength fluctuations.
elements.sent-2411-109: In field theory, this process is well-defined as long as the theory includes a canonical notion of scale.
elements.sent-2411-110: In QFT, this scale is often derived from the background space or space-time, which provides canonical definitions of microscopic and macroscopic physics, allowing wavelength modes to be uniquely ordered by these boundaries \cite{ZinnJustinBook1 .}
elements.sent-2411-111: The RG flow can also be defined in a more abstract framework that does not depend on background structures like space-time, as long as quantum fluctuations can be sized.
elements.sent-2411-112: Here, this size is determined by the spectrum of the Gaussian kernel, with fluctuations, in the corresponding eigenbasis, ordered by the size of their eigenvalues \cite{lahoche2022generalized, Bradde, Lahochebeyond, CarrozzaReview .}

elements.sent-2411-115: In the field theory we consider in this paper, there exists a one-dimensional background manifold, time, that provides a canonical notion of scale.
elements.sent-2411-116: Consequently, a renormalization group can, in principle, be constructed through coarse-graining over frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-117: Such coarse-graining has recently been studied in contexts like classical spin glass kinetics \cite{lahoche2023functional, lahoche2022functional , out-of-equilibrium field theories, such as model A and the Kardar-Parisi-Zhang (KPZ) equation \cite{duclut2017frequency, squizzato2019kardar , and quantum mechanical systems \cite{zappala2001improving, synatschke2009flow, heilmann2015convergence .}}}
elements.sent-2411-118: However, in this paper, we consider a more abstract RG approach, based on coarse-graining over the spectrum of the kinetic disorder $K$, specifically over the generalized momentum—see Figure \ref{figRG1 .}
elements.sent-2411-119: Unlike frequencies, this spectrum does not relate to any background scaling and serves as an example of the abstract scaling mentioned earlier.
elements.sent-2411-120: Moreover, it provides a canonical notion of ‘‘what is ultraviolet" (UV) and ‘‘what is infrared" (IR), corresponding respectively to the regions $p^2 \approx 4\sigma$ and $p^2 \approx 0$.
elements.sent-2411-121: We can then construct a coarse-grained description of the underlying IR effective physics by partially integrating out UV fluctuations associated with large generalized momenta.
elements.sent-2411-122: One could also consider a ‘‘mixed" coarse-graining approach, incorporating both frequency and momentum.
elements.sent-2411-123: In similar cases, this mixed approximation has been shown to offer significant qualitative benefits \cite{duclut2017frequency .}
elements.sent-2411-124: However, our objective in this paper is to develop the formalism, so we simplify the process by focusing solely on coarse-graining over the generalized momentum, reserving a more detailed examination of the mixed approach for a forthcoming paper \cite{lahoche2024frequency .}
elements.sent-2411-125: This approach has been explored in our previous work \cite{lahoche2024largetimeeffectivekinetics and in other studies across various contexts \cite{duclut2017nonuniversality, guilleux2015quantum, canet2010nonperturbative, canet2011nonperturbative, canet2016fully .}}

elements.sent-2411-127: In the literature, there exist several popular ‘‘exact" equations that describe how the physical representation of a field theory changes as degrees of freedom within specific momentum windows are integrated out.
elements.sent-2411-128: The Polchinski equation \cite{polchinski1984renormalization, ZinnJustinBook1 is an exact equation that describes how the classical (bare) action changes during each infinitesimal RG step.}
elements.sent-2411-129: It serves as a formally exact equation and a deep mathematical realization of Wilson's RG.
elements.sent-2411-130: However, there is another, less widely known approach developed by Wetterich and Morris \cite{bagnuls2001exact, Delamotte_2012, Berges, dupuis2021nonperturbative .}
elements.sent-2411-131: This approach, known as the effective average action (EAA) method, differs from Wilson's perspective in the choice of mathematical object.
elements.sent-2411-132: Instead of focusing on the classical action, the Wetterich approach centers on the ‘‘effective action of integrated-out modes." Furthermore, the EAA approach differs from Wilson's in how the fundamental cut-off is applied.
elements.sent-2411-133: In the Wilson-Polchinski framework, the bare action is varied, and the fundamental cut-off around which fluctuations are integrated is rescaled at each RG step.
elements.sent-2411-134: In contrast, within the EAA formalism, the fundamental cut-off and the bare action remain fixed, with contributions from IR fluctuations to the full effective action being gradually suppressed until reaching an IR cut-off.
elements.sent-2411-135: The EAA formalism offers several advantages over the Wilson-Polchinski formalism, particularly for nonperturbative analysis and studies of phases with broken symmetries.
elements.sent-2411-136: Notably, this approach helps regularize the infrared divergences that typically arise in the broken symmetry region when the mass becomes negative.
elements.sent-2411-137: Let us proceed with the explicit construction of the formalism.

elements.sent-2411-516: In this paper, we have investigated the behavior of the RG flow for a “$2 + p$” quantum spin glass constructed by integrating out degrees of freedom labeled by the eigenvalues of the matrix-like disorder spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-517: In our approach, the effective coupling constants are defined with a special normalization that ensures the effective number of “sites” on the underlying network (representing the system’s volume) remains constant.
elements.sent-2411-518: More precisely, we take the infinite volume limit before performing the coarse-graining.
elements.sent-2411-519: This paper has three main results.
elements.sent-2411-520: The first pertains to a non-trivial Ward identity arising from the gauge fixing required in constructing the RG flow, which breaks the model's underlying $O(N)$ invariance.
elements.sent-2411-521: Due to these Ward identities, the flow of the field strength renormalization $Z(k)$ in the symmetric phase is entirely determined by the order $\mathcal{O (N^0)$ RG flow of the quartic coupling.}
elements.sent-2411-522: The second result concerns the EVE method, which allows us to close the hierarchy around sextic interactions in the symmetric phase, working at the leading order of the derivative expansion.
elements.sent-2411-523: This method, initially introduced for tensorial field theories \cite{Lahochebeyond , is closely related to our topic, especially regarding the theory’s specific non-locality.}
elements.sent-2411-524: Our conclusions are similar to those in our previous work \cite{lahoche2024largetimeeffectivekinetics , where we show finite-scale singularities due to disorder, interpreted as a signal that something is missing from the perturbative theory.}
elements.sent-2411-525: The main steps of this approximation's construction are detailed in the companion paper \cite{lahoche2024frequency .}
elements.sent-2411-526: The third and primary contribution of this paper is a formalism aimed at going beyond the vertex expansion.
elements.sent-2411-527: This approach resembles a formal expansion around the vacuum of the local potential approximation, where the local potential flow is “dressed” by the flow of non-local interactions.
elements.sent-2411-528: We derive the flow equation for the various couplings, focusing on the next-to-leading order beyond the just-renormalizable sector and examining asymptotic fixed-point solutions.
elements.sent-2411-529: Our numerical investigations indicate that divergences in the symmetric phase cancel out, and where these divergences occur, the flow of non-local couplings becomes relevant—contrary to what is typically expected from perturbation theory.
elements.sent-2411-530: These results provide strong evidence of a phase transition between a regime where replicas are uncorrelated and one where their correlations are non-zero.
elements.sent-2411-531: The main open challenge is reconstructing the complete theory space from the RG perspective to achieve our overarching goal: understanding the glassy transition via RG.
elements.sent-2411-532: Many intermediate results are essential on this path, particularly regarding the physical properties of the transition.
elements.sent-2411-533: Our upcoming work will focus on the 2PI formalism, which is particularly suited when the $2$-point function serves as the order parameter.
elements.sent-2411-534: Lastly, here are some open issues we plan to address in future work:.
elements.sent-2411-535: Evaluating the reliability of the vertex expansion, extending beyond the leading order of the derivative expansion.
elements.sent-2411-536: Considering coarse-graining in both eigenvalue and time dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-537: Developing approximations valid beyond the deep IR, going beyond the derivative expansion.
elements.sent-2411-538: Assessing the accuracy of the derivative expansion by computing anomalous dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-539: More systematically incorporating non-local interactions in truncations.
elements.sent-2411-540: Investigating the breaking of time translation invariance symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-541: Additionally, we might consider the UV regime, where the non-trivial scaling due to coarse-graining over the Wigner spectrum could play a significant role.
elements.sent-2411-542: Specifically, outside the critical regime investigated in previous work \cite{lahoche2024largetimeeffectivekinetics , the canonical dimension depends on the relevant couplings $u_2$ and $q^\prime$, presenting a novel challenge in constructing the effective phase space.}
